retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mosaicism -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Somatic mosaicism may occur as a result of any type of mutation, ranging from chromosomal abnormalities to single nucleotide alterations. Mosaic aneuploidies are generated by two principal mechanisms (Image 2): post-zygotic mitotic non-disjunction or post-zygotic mitotic trisomy rescue after a meiotic non-disjunction. The second indicates a biological attempt to restore the normal chromosome number, which is known as a chromosomal rescue . This mechanism is achieved through the loss of one random supernumerary chromosome in a somatic cell, restoring the euploid state. Nonetheless, chromosome rescue constitutes an increased risk for uniparental disomy, thereby interfering with genomic imprinting and elevating the likelihood of homozygosity for a recessive mutation. [5] [6] [43] Other mechanisms that can cause mosaicism include anaphase lagging and endoreplication. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Angelman Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: AS is typically caused by a sporadic de novo mutation on the maternal gene UBE3A (part of the ubiquitin pathway) at chromosome 15q11-13. Around 70 to 75% of cases arise from maternal deletion within chromosome 15q11-q13, containing this gene. Approximately 2% to 3% can be due to paternal uniparental disomy (UPD), imprinting center defect (3% to 5%), or single gene point mutation in the maternal UBE3A allele (5 to 10%). [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Prader-Willi Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: PWS results from the absence of gene expression of the paternally inherited genes on the 15q11.2-q13 chromosome. Approximately 70% of cases result from errors in genomic imprinting due to a paternal deletion, while maternal uniparental disomy is responsible for about 25% of cases. Fewer cases stem from defects in the imprinting center, such as microdeletions or epimutations on chromosome 15. [1] [2] While most occurrences of PWS are sporadic, familial instances may occur when paternal genes carry a microdeletion in the imprinting center, which is inherited from the paternal grandmother. PWS was the first genetic disorder identified to be caused by genomic imprinting, where the expression of a gene depends on the parental sex that donated the affected gene.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mosaicism -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Some investigators suggest that mosaicism may be a potential protective survival mechanism. Based on the fact that only a few autosomal and sex aneuploidies are compatible with human life (i.e., trisomy of chromosomes 13, 18, 21), a broader range of aneuploidies has been described in the mosaic state. For example, mosaic trisomies reported in the literature include chromosomes 8 (Warkany syndrome), 14, 16, and 17. Furthermore, while autosomal monosomy in humans is considered essentially lethal, with only a couple of cases reported for monosomy 21, a mosaic state worth noting involves isochromosome 12p, also known as Pallister–Killian syndrome. Survival is hypothetically possible due to the presence of a sufficient proportion of healthy cells. [5] [6] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mosaicism -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Mosaicism affects the survival rates in monosomies : In humans, monosomies are fatal, but mosaic monosomies (in case more than 70% of cells are normal and remaining are monosomes) may survive after birth. [68] [69]

retrieved_docs.title: Edwards Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Complete trisomy 18 is the most common form (94%). In this type, every cell contains 3 complete copies of chromosome 18. The extra chromosome is due to nondisjunction, mostly during meiosis II. The extra chromosome is most often of maternal origin. The frequency of nondisjunction errors increases with advancing maternal age. [3] Mosaic trisomy 18 is the second most common type (<5%). In this type, both a complete trisomy 18 and a normal cell line exist. Thus, the phenotype can range from a complete trisomy 18 phenotype with early mortality to a normal phenotype. Partial trisomy 18 accounts for 2% of Edwards syndrome. In this type, only a partial segment of chromosome 18q is present in triplicate. The partial triplicate often results from a balanced translocation or inversion carried by 1 of the parents. The phenotype of partial trisomy 18 is variable based on the location and extent of the triplicated segment.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Chromosome Abnormalities -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, an aneuploid chromosome set varies from the wild type by a small number of chromosomes (typically one). For example, trisomy (three copies of a specific homologous chromosome or 2N + 1) is the most frequent constitutional chromosomal abnormality in humans. [5] Research has identified an extra chromosome copy for every chromosome, but most of them are lethal during embryogenesis; even the known viable clinical syndromes for trisomies are more frequently observed in spontaneous abortions than in live births. [5] [6] Moreover, monosomies (a single non-homologous set of chromosomes or 2N - 1) are remarkably rare in humans and practically limited to chromosomes 21 or X. These findings have led to the hypothesis that the consequences of such polyploidies are the product of an imbalance from critical gene products encoded on, what could only be, small gene-poor chromosomes. [5] [6] Of note, numerical chromosomal abnormalities in sex chromosomes have different implications than in autosomes. For instance, aneuploidy in autosomes are less common than in sex chromosome, and trisomies seem to be tolerated better than monosomies. [1] This explains why having an extra or missing sex chromosome is more compatible with life and may have only milder consequences (trisomy) or equally severe consequences (monosomy) than autosome aneuploidies, as discussed later.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Nondisjunction -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Meiotic nondisjunction is of greater clinical significance since most aneuploidies are incompatible with life. However, some will result in viable offspring with a spectrum of developmental disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Meiosis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, errors in meiosis can create many life-threatening outcomes. The most common error of meiosis is nondisjunction, when chromatids fail to separate during either anaphase I or II, creating imbalances in the number of chromosomes in each daughter cell. Most imbalances are incompatible with life, but some will result in viable offspring with a spectrum of developmental disorders. These medical conditions include Down syndrome, Patau syndrome, Edwards syndrome, Klinefelter syndrome, Turner syndrome, Triple X syndrome, and XYY syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mosaicism -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Euploid-aneuploid mosaic relates to a mixture of euploid and aneuploid cell populations in an individual. Some cells of the body are aneuploid (45 or 47 chromosomes), while some are euploid (46 chromosomes). The severity of the clinical condition depends on the proportion of the euploid cells. This mosaicism is more common than as the embryo has more chances to survive during intrauterine life. [11] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Beckwith-Wiedemann Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Methylation, which silences gene expression, usually occurs in the paternal allele at IC1 and the maternal allele at IC2. In an individual with BWS, the molecular defects commonly described are the following: [4] Loss of methylation at IC2 on the maternal allele in 50% to 60% of cases Paternal uniparental isodisomy of chromosome 11p15 in 20% to 25% of cases IC1 methylation on the maternal allele in 5 to 10% of cases Autosomal dominant maternal point mutations in IC2-regulated CDKN1C in 5% of sporadic cases and 40% of familial cases Chromosomal rearrangements, ie, duplications, translocations, deletions, or inversions, in fewer than 1% of cases An unknown molecular defect in 10% to 15% of cases

retrieved_docs.title: Patau Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cause of Patau syndrome is the presence of three copies of chromosome 13; this is due most commonly to nondisjunction in meiosis, occurring more frequently in mothers of advanced age (age greater than 35). [1] Another cause is an unbalanced Robertsonian translocation, which results in two normal copies of chromosome 13 and an additional long arm of chromosome 13. [1] Another less common cause is mosaicism, which results in 3 copies of chromosome 13 in some cells and two copies in the others. Mosaicism is the outcome of a mitotic nondisjunction error and is unrelated to maternal age. [3] Prognosis is better in patients with mosaicism and patients with unbalanced translocations. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Cri Du Chat Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cri-du chat results from either a partial or complete deletion of chromosome 5p. Most of the deletions occur de novo . The deletions occur as random events during the formation of reproductive cells in early fetal development. Around 80% to 90% are paternal in origin, which can arise from a chromosomal breakage during gamete formation. The remaining 10% to 15% result from unbalanced parental translocation. Moreover, 80% to 90% of cases result from terminal deletions of chromosome 5, while 3% to 5% are due to an interstitial deletion. Mosaicism, inversions, and ring chromosomes are less common mechanisms. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Angelman Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Inherited causes of AS are mostly either due to UBE3A mutation (mainly in the mother's paternally inherited allele), which is inherited by the baby and a subtype of imprinting defects called submicroscopic deletion of imprinting center. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Trisomy 13 -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Trisomy 13 arises from the nondisjunction of germ cells during meiosis I or II of either parental cells. [5] Nonetheless, maternal germ cell nondisjunction correlated to the increased age of conception contributes to 91% of cases. [3] The mode of inheritance for the complete trisomy 13 is caused by spontaneous interference in meiosis, while vertical inheritance is hereditary in balanced translocations. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Given the X-linked inheritance pattern of muscular dystrophy, the overwhelming majority of patients are male. [4] Symptomatic disease in daughters can be explained by factors such as Turner syndrome, skewed X chromosome inactivation, translocation of the mutated gene to an autosome, or uniparental disomy (both copies of a chromosome set originating from one parent). [80] Typically, symptomatic females present in infancy with proximal muscle weakness. [81] Reports exist of increased weakness in adulthood, myalgias, spasms, and lethargy as initial manifestations. [75] Scoliosis and sustained alveolar hypoventilation can cause severe problems for every child with muscular dystrophy. [82]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: to the broader concept of fetal distress that influences maternal treatment (ضائقة جنينية تؤثر في علاج الأم), highlighting the bidirectional impact of fetal and maternal conditions. Maternal pregnancy complications as a whole are nested within a larger, unclassified group of maternal complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر), emphasizing that many of these disorders are interrelated and may require coordinated assessment, risk stratification, and management across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2924
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with gestation twin, indicating that information systems and data repositories are linked to the clinical aspects and management of twin pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2035
retrieved_docs.content: Circumvallate Placenta Syndrome is a condition that is directly related to the placenta. Both marginal placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage and partial placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage are identified as having the placenta as the primary site of pathology. The placenta itself can present in several anatomical configurations, including multipartite (multilobed) and tripartite (three‑lobed) forms, as well as a displaced placenta that occurs without bleeding. Pathological findings associated with the placenta encompass placental insufficiency, fetal‑maternal hemorrhage syndrome, and combined maternal and fetal hemorrhage. Recognizing these interconnections among placental disorders, their locations, and associated hemorrhagic complications is essential for accurate diagnosis and optimal obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
retrieved_docs.content: The term “اضطراب جنيني” (fetal disorder) is mapped from “متلازمة الضائقة الجنينية” (fetal distress syndrome), indicating that the clinical entity described as fetal distress syndrome corresponds to or is represented by the label fetal disorder in certain contexts. This mapping clarifies terminology, ensuring consistent reference to the same condition across medical documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: lesions, fetal demise, and undefined obstetric indications within a comprehensive framework that guides clinicians in assessing how each factor may affect maternal and fetal outcomes and informs comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 93
retrieved_docs.content: abnormalities, treatment‑related complications, and a wide array of multilingual disease entities into a comprehensive classification system that supports accurate diagnosis, coding, reporting, and patient management across diverse medical domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1636
retrieved_docs.content: abnormalities (Gestation abnormality) are linked as a downstream condition of bleeding in pregnancy, indicating that hemorrhagic events can contribute to broader developmental disturbances of the pregnancy. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how various placental positions, coagulation abnormalities, timing of hemorrhage, and associated hematologic findings converge under the single clinical concept of bleeding in pregnancy, emphasizing the importance of recognizing each subtype for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management in obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: that relate to fetopelvic disproportion; obstruction and malposition; malpresentation, respectively. Collectively, these entities and relationships emphasize the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment, careful monitoring of fetal presentation and pelvic dimensions, avoidance of contraindicated medications, and individualized delivery planning to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2019
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium Terminology defines a specific subset that includes concepts related to digestive congenital anomalies, indicating that within the CDISC framework these anomalies are organized as a distinct category to support standardized representation of associated clinical concepts.
